text: There is a town I think about where the road gives out at the edge of a salt marsh, and the last house before the water belongs to no one anyone can name. Its porch sags toward the reeds, and in late afternoon the light comes in low and copper, turning the standing water to hammered metal between the grasses. Herons stand in it like they are waiting for someone to arrive, and the wind carries that particular smell of brine and decaying sweetness that means the tide is going out. You can hear the rigging of small boats clinking somewhere you cannot see, and the whole place feels suspended, as though it has agreed to stop just short of becoming the sea.
